## Structure (high-level)

Preface; thirteen substantive chapters; Appendix A (Financial Statement Analysis — substantive); Appendix B (Time Value of Money tables — reference lookup); Appendix C (Suggested Resources — reference list); Answer Key; Index.

Chapters:

1. Accounting as a Tool for Managers
2. Building Blocks of Managerial Accounting
3. Cost-Volume-Profit Analysis
4. Job Order Costing
5. Process Costing
6. Activity-Based, Variable, and Absorption Costing
7. Budgeting
8. Standard Costs and Variances
9. Responsibility Accounting and Decentralization
10. Short-Term Decision-Making
11. Capital Budgeting Decisions
12. Balanced Scorecard and Other Performance Measures
13. Sustainability Reporting

## Citation style decision

`(Ch N, "Section name")`. The PDF was converted with pymupdf4llm 0.2.9, which does not preserve page anchors; section names from the converted markdown are used in their place.

## Coverage decision (Option B — substantive content only)

Per global decision Q2, the deep ref covers chapter exposition only. Per-chapter scaffolding sections excluded:

- Key Terms (and analogues: Glossary, etc.)
- Summary / Summary of Learning Outcomes / Chapter Summary
- Multiple Choice
- Questions
- Exercise Set A / Exercise Set B
- Problem Set A / Problem Set B
- Thought Provokers
- Per-chapter footnotes / endnotes (cited inline only when relevant to a quoted passage)

Included: Why It Matters chapter introductions; all numbered substantive sections (e.g., 1.1-1.5, 2.1-2.3, etc.); embedded features (Concepts in Practice, Continuing Application, Ethical Considerations, Think It Through, Your Turn, IFRS Connection, Link to Learning) where they expand on substantive content. Appendix A is included as substantive (Financial Statement Analysis covers ratios and analytical techniques). Appendices B (PV/FV tables) and C (Suggested Resources) and the Answer Key and Index are excluded as reference/scaffolding back-matter.

## Pass B structural read — internal map

### Named cases / worked examples

- Daryn's Dairy (organic ice cream producer; Ch 1, 2)
- Big Bad Bikes (bicycle / trainer manufacturer; Ch 7 — focal worked example for budgeting chain)
- Hicks Manufacturing (Blue Jay birdbaths; Ch 3 — focal CVP worked example)
- Dinosaur Vinyl (sign manufacturer; Ch 4 — focal job-order worked example)
- Rock City Percussion (drumstick manufacturer; Ch 5 — focal process-costing worked example)
- Musicality Manufacturing (recording-device producer; Ch 6 — focal ABC worked example)
- Connie's Candy Company (candy / candy-box producer; Ch 8 — focal variances worked example)
- Apparel World (department store; Ch 9 — focal responsibility-centre worked example)
- Scrumptious Sweets (bakery with three divisions: donut, brownie, bagel; Ch 12 — focal performance-measure worked example)
- Carolina Yachts (sportfishing-boat manufacturer; Ch 2)
- Ocean Breeze (resort hotel; Ch 2 — mixed-cost example)
- Tony's T-Shirts (screen-printing company; Ch 2 — stepped-cost example)
- Beach Inn (small hotel; Ch 2 — high-low example)
- Regent Airlines (regional jets; Ch 2 — scatter-graph example)
- Wood World (wooden desks/chairs/bookcases; Ch 10 — constraint-management example)
- Rudolph Incorporated / Rayford Machining (Ch 11 — discounted cash flow examples)
- Melanie's Sewing Studio, BGM (Ch 11)
- Real-company illustrations: Apple, Boeing, Toyota, Ford, Walmart, Tesla, Coca-Cola, Patagonia, Nestlé, Union Carbide, Johnson & Johnson, Bank of America, Coal India, China Mobile, Volkswagen, Qualcomm, Procter & Gamble, NASA, UPS, Wells Fargo, Hilton Hotels, New Belgium Brewing, Gearhead Outfitters (Continuing Application), Just Born Peeps, Nabisco Oreos, Levi's, Star Wars / Logan film budgets, Pet Smart, H&R Block, Marshalls, Chili's, Disney, GE.

### Recurring motifs

- Strategic plan → goals → controls → evaluate (Ch 1, 7, 12).
- Internal users / external users distinction (Ch 1, 4, 13).
- Direct materials / direct labour / manufacturing overhead trio (Chs 2, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8).
- Variable cost / fixed cost / mixed cost / step cost (Ch 2, 3, 6, 7, 10).
- Relevant range as the bounds within which fixed costs hold (Ch 2, 3, 7, 10).
- Contribution margin as the bridge between cost behaviour and CVP / decision-making (Ch 3, 6, 7, 10).
- Make-or-buy / sell-or-process / keep-or-discontinue / special-order / constrained-resource as the canonical short-term decision categories (Ch 10).
- Time value of money as the dividing line between short-term and long-term decision frames (Ch 10 vs Ch 11).
- Stakeholders, sustainability, triple bottom line, materiality, GRI/SASB/Integrated Reporting (Ch 1.5, Ch 13).
- Ethics: IMA Statement of Ethical Professional Practice (competence, confidentiality, integrity, credibility); SOX; FCPA; bribery; whistleblowers (Ch 1.4 and recurring Ethical Considerations).

### Author's signature contrarian / contestable positions

The text is a textbook, so it generally presents consensus content rather than personal positions. The strongest positional claims are:

- Variable costing provides better information for internal decision-making than absorption costing, even though only absorption costing is GAAP-compliant (Ch 6.5).
- Allocated common costs can mislead profitability analysis at the segment level; segment elimination decisions should use the contribution-margin approach to avoid misleading allocation effects (Ch 10.4).
- ROI as a sole evaluation measure can produce decisions that benefit a segment but harm the company; residual income (and EVA) better aligns segment decisions with company-wide value (Ch 9.3, Ch 12.3).
- The balanced scorecard's claim is that financial measures alone produce short-term, distortion-prone evaluation; quantitative-and-qualitative across four perspectives is required (Ch 12.4).
- Sustainability reporting is presented as a mainstream rather than a fringe practice: 93 per cent of the world's 250 largest companies by revenue produced corporate responsibility reports in 2017 (Ch 13.3).

### Cross-author citations the source makes

The text is light on author attribution (it is a textbook, not a research monograph). Named theorists and practitioners cited in the source:

- Eliyahu Goldratt (theory of constraints, Ch 1.5; bottleneck management implicit Ch 10.6)
- John Elkington (triple bottom line, Ch 13.1)
- Gro Harlem Brundtland (Brundtland Report 1987, Ch 13.1)
- Robert Kaplan and David Norton (balanced scorecard, Ch 12.4)
- Art Schneiderman (early balanced scorecard at Analog Devices, Ch 12.4)
- Chris Argyris (double loop learning, Ch 9.1 sidebar)
- Yvon Chouinard / Patagonia (B-corp founder, Ch 13.1)
- Klaus Schwab (technological revolution, Ch 13.4)
- Bill Gates (robot tax, Ch 13.4)
- Yanis Varoufakis (UBI / UBD, Ch 13.4)
- Toyota / Taiichi Ohno (lean manufacturing / JIT, Ch 1.5)
- Motorola (Lean Six Sigma origin, Ch 1.5)
- Dr Friedman / Dr Rosenman (not in this text — Vol 1 / OB context only)
- US Supreme Court (TSC Indus. v. Northway materiality definition adopted by SASB, Ch 13.3)
- Mary Parker Follett (not cited in Vol 2; cited in OB)
- IMA (Institute of Management Accountants, Ch 1.4)
- IFRS / IASB (Ch 1 IFRS Connection)
- Ulrich and Sandy Jenkins (Collin Street Bakery fraud, Ch 5)
- Sandy Walker / Gary Fleischman (ethics in budgeting study, Ch 7.5 footnote)
- Michael Hitt and Jamie Collins (pro-stakeholder culture, Ch 9.4 footnote)
- Jeffrey R. Cohen and Laurie W. Pant (cost accounting ethics, Ch 8 footnote)
- Yvon Chouinard / Tin Shed Ventures (Ch 13.1)
